comment_text.sn41.8:1.1: This is Mahāvīra, who according to Buddhist accounts died before the Buddha, so if this is a genuine encounter it must have been while the Buddha was still alive. The whole story seems a little parodic, although Citta’s eagerness to trounce Mahāvīra is very much in character.
comment_text.sn41.8:2.4: It is not easy to trace details of early Jain meditation. For the most part they focused on austerities and self-mortification, which the Buddha found to be counterproductive to deep meditation (MN 36:33.2).
comment_text.sn41.8:3.3: Citta uses the same technique employed so often by the Buddha: start by finding something in common. The superiority of knowledge over faith is a philosophical point in which Buddhism and Jainism are in agreement.
comment_text.sn41.8:5.9: This passage echoes AN 10.27:9.2, where the Buddha recommends asking this series of questions of a sectarian in order to stump them. | The grammar of these questions is peculiar, for which see Bodhi’s note 323 in Connected Discourses and 2003 in Numerical Discourses.
comment_text.sn41.8:5.19: This whole passage is, I think, Pali for: “You are so pwned. Delete your account.”

root_text.sn41.8:1.1: Tena kho pana samayena nigaṇṭho nāṭaputto macchikāsaṇḍaṁ anuppatto hoti mahatiyā nigaṇṭhaparisāya saddhiṁ.
root_text.sn41.8:1.2: Assosi kho citto gahapati: “nigaṇṭho kira nāṭaputto macchikāsaṇḍaṁ anuppatto mahatiyā nigaṇṭhaparisāya saddhin”ti.
root_text.sn41.8:1.3: Atha kho citto gahapati sambahulehi upāsakehi saddhiṁ yena nigaṇṭho nāṭaputto tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā nigaṇṭhena nāṭaputtena saddhiṁ sammodi.
root_text.sn41.8:1.4: Sammodanīyaṁ kathaṁ sāraṇīyaṁ vītisāretvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinnaṁ kho cittaṁ gahapatiṁ nigaṇṭho nāṭaputto etadavoca:
root_text.sn41.8:1.6: atthi avitakko avicāro samādhi, atthi vitakkavicārānaṁ nirodho”ti?
root_text.sn41.8:2.1: “Na khvāhaṁ ettha, bhante, bhagavato saddhāya gacchāmi.
root_text.sn41.8:2.2: Atthi avitakko avicāro samādhi, atthi vitakkavicārānaṁ nirodho”ti.
root_text.sn41.8:2.3: Evaṁ vutte, nigaṇṭho nāṭaputto ulloketvā etadavoca:
root_text.sn41.8:2.4: “idaṁ bhavanto passantu, yāva ujuko cāyaṁ citto gahapati, yāva asaṭho cāyaṁ citto gahapati, yāva amāyāvī cāyaṁ citto gahapati, vātaṁ vā so jālena bādhetabbaṁ maññeyya, yo vitakkavicāre nirodhetabbaṁ maññeyya, sakamuṭṭhinā vā so gaṅgāya sotaṁ āvāretabbaṁ maññeyya, yo vitakkavicāre nirodhetabbaṁ maññeyyā”ti.
root_text.sn41.8:3.2: katamaṁ nu kho paṇītataraṁ—ñāṇaṁ vā saddhā vā”ti?
root_text.sn41.8:3.4: “Ahaṁ kho, bhante, yāvadeva ākaṅkhāmi, vivicceva kāmehi vivicca akusalehi dhammehi savitakkaṁ savicāraṁ vivekajaṁ pītisukhaṁ paṭhamaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharāmi.
root_text.sn41.8:3.5: Ahaṁ kho, bhante, yāvadeva ākaṅkhāmi, vitakkavicārānaṁ vūpasamā …pe… dutiyaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharāmi.
root_text.sn41.8:3.6: Ahaṁ kho, bhante, yāvadeva ākaṅkhāmi, pītiyā ca virāgā …pe… tatiyaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharāmi.
root_text.sn41.8:3.7: Ahaṁ kho, bhante, yāvadeva ākaṅkhāmi, sukhassa ca pahānā …pe… catutthaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharāmi.
root_text.sn41.8:3.8: Na so khvāhaṁ, bhante, evaṁ jānanto evaṁ passanto kassa aññassa samaṇassa vā brāhmaṇassa vā saddhāya gamissāmi?
root_text.sn41.8:3.9: Atthi avitakko avicāro samādhi, atthi vitakkavicārānaṁ nirodho”ti.
root_text.sn41.8:4.1: Evaṁ vutte, nigaṇṭho nāṭaputto sakaṁ parisaṁ apaloketvā etadavoca:
root_text.sn41.8:4.2: “idaṁ bhavanto passantu, yāva anujuko cāyaṁ citto gahapati, yāva saṭho cāyaṁ citto gahapati, yāva māyāvī cāyaṁ citto gahapatī”ti.
root_text.sn41.8:5.2: ‘evaṁ ājānāma idaṁ bhavanto passantu, yāva ujuko cāyaṁ citto gahapati, yāva asaṭho cāyaṁ citto gahapati, yāva amāyāvī cāyaṁ citto gahapatī’ti.
root_text.sn41.8:5.3: Idāneva ca pana mayaṁ, bhante, bhāsitaṁ:
root_text.sn41.8:5.4: ‘evaṁ ājānāma idaṁ bhavanto passantu, yāva anujuko cāyaṁ citto gahapati, yāva saṭho cāyaṁ citto gahapati, yāva māyāvī cāyaṁ citto gahapatī’ti.
root_text.sn41.8:5.5: Sace te, bhante, purimaṁ saccaṁ, pacchimaṁ te micchā.
root_text.sn41.8:5.6: Sace pana te, bhante, purimaṁ micchā, pacchimaṁ te saccaṁ.
root_text.sn41.8:5.7: Ime kho pana, bhante, dasa sahadhammikā pañhā āgacchanti.
root_text.sn41.8:5.8: Yadā nesaṁ atthaṁ ājāneyyāsi, atha maṁ paṭihareyyāsi saddhiṁ nigaṇṭhaparisāya.
root_text.sn41.8:5.9: Eko pañho, eko uddeso, ekaṁ veyyākaraṇaṁ.
root_text.sn41.8:5.10: Dve pañhā, dve uddesā, dve veyyākaraṇāni.
root_text.sn41.8:5.11: Tayo pañhā, tayo uddesā, tīṇi veyyākaraṇāni.
root_text.sn41.8:5.12: Cattāro pañhā, cattāro uddesā, cattāri veyyākaraṇāni.
root_text.sn41.8:5.13: Pañca pañhā, pañca uddesā, pañca veyyākaraṇāni.
root_text.sn41.8:5.14: Cha pañhā, cha uddesā, cha veyyākaraṇāni.
root_text.sn41.8:5.15: Satta pañhā, satta uddesā, satta veyyākaraṇāni.
root_text.sn41.8:5.16: Aṭṭha pañhā, aṭṭha uddesā, aṭṭha veyyākaraṇāni.
root_text.sn41.8:5.17: Nava pañhā, nava uddesā, nava veyyākaraṇāni.
root_text.sn41.8:5.18: Dasa pañhā, dasa uddesā, dasa veyyākaraṇānī”ti.
root_text.sn41.8:5.19: Atha kho citto gahapati nigaṇṭhaṁ nāṭaputtaṁ ime dasa sahadhammike pañhe āpucchitvā uṭṭhāyāsanā pakkāmīti.

translation_text.sn41.8:0.3: The Jain Ascetic of the Ñātika Clan
translation_text.sn41.8:1.1: Now at that time the Jain ascetic of the Ñātika clan had arrived at Macchikāsaṇḍa together with a large assembly of Jain ascetics.
translation_text.sn41.8:1.2: Citta the householder heard that they had arrived.
translation_text.sn41.8:1.3: Together with several lay followers, he went up to the Jain Ñātika and exchanged greetings with him.
translation_text.sn41.8:1.4: When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side. The Jain Ñātika said to him,
translation_text.sn41.8:1.5: “Householder, do you have faith in the ascetic Gotama’s claim that
translation_text.sn41.8:1.6: there is a state of immersion without placing the mind and keeping it connected; that there is the cessation of placing the mind and keeping it connected?”
translation_text.sn41.8:2.1: “Sir, in this case I don’t rely on faith in the Buddha’s claim that
translation_text.sn41.8:2.2: there is a state of immersion without placing the mind and keeping it connected; that there is the cessation of placing the mind and keeping it connected.”
translation_text.sn41.8:2.3: When he said this, the Jain Ñātika looked up at his assembly and said,
translation_text.sn41.8:2.4: “See, good fellows, how straightforward this householder Citta is! He’s not devious or deceitful at all. To imagine that you can stop placing the mind and keeping it connected would be like imagining that you can catch the wind in a net, or dam the Ganges river with your own hand.”
translation_text.sn41.8:3.3: “Knowledge is definitely better than faith, householder.”
translation_text.sn41.8:3.4: “Well sir, whenever I want, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, I enter and remain in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected.
translation_text.sn41.8:3.5: And whenever I want, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled … I enter and remain in the second absorption.
translation_text.sn41.8:3.6: And whenever I want, with the fading away of rapture … I enter and remain in the third absorption.
translation_text.sn41.8:3.7: And whenever I want, giving up pleasure and pain … I enter and remain in the fourth absorption.
translation_text.sn41.8:3.8: And so, sir, since I know and see like this, why should I rely on faith in another ascetic or brahmin who claims that
translation_text.sn41.8:3.9: there is a state of immersion without placing the mind and keeping it connected; that there is the cessation of placing the mind and keeping it connected?”
translation_text.sn41.8:4.1: When he said this, the Jain Ñātika looked askance at his own assembly and said,
translation_text.sn41.8:4.2: “See, good fellows, how crooked this householder Citta is! He’s so devious and deceitful!”
translation_text.sn41.8:5.1: “Sir, just now I understood you to say:
translation_text.sn41.8:5.2: ‘See, good fellows, how straightforward this householder Citta is! He’s not devious or deceitful at all.’
translation_text.sn41.8:5.3: But then I understood you to say:
translation_text.sn41.8:5.4: ‘See, good fellows, how crooked this householder Citta is! He’s so devious and deceitful!’
translation_text.sn41.8:5.5: If your first statement is true, the second is wrong.
translation_text.sn41.8:5.6: If your first statement is wrong, the second is true.
translation_text.sn41.8:5.7: And also, sir, these ten legitimate questions are relevant.
translation_text.sn41.8:5.8: When you understand what they mean, then, together with your assembly of Jain ascetics, you can rebut me.
translation_text.sn41.8:5.18: Ten things: question, summary, and answer.’”
translation_text.sn41.8:5.19: Then Citta got up from his seat and left without asking the Jain Ñātika these ten legitimate questions.

variant_text.sn41.8:2.3: ulloketvā → sakaṁ parisaṁ ulloketvā (bj, pts1ed); sakaṁ parisaṁ apaloketvā (bj, sya-all, km); oloketvā (bj-a, sya-a)
variant_text.sn41.8:5.1: Idāneva kho te → idāneva ca pana (sya-all, km, mr); idāneva kho te pana (pts1ed)
